posts.hook: Saccades: the eye movement exercise you've never heard of — and why athletes swear by it.
posts.caption:
Saccades = rapid, precise eye jumps between fixed points.

Your eyes do this 3–5 times per second when reading.

When saccades are slow or inaccurate:
- You re-read lines
- Lose your place
- Reading feels exhausting

Saccades exercises improve:
- Reading speed
- Sports reaction time
- Driving safety

Guided saccades exercise: axeo.vision/exercise

#Saccades #EyeExercise #EyeHealth #Axeo #HealthTok #VisionTraining #AthleteEyeHealth

posts.hook: Saccades training — the 3-minute exercise that improves reading speed and sports performance.
posts.caption:
Saccades are the rapid, precise eye jumps your brain makes when reading, driving, or playing sports.

When your saccades are accurate:
- Faster reading
- Less re-reading
- Better reaction time
- Improved sports tracking

Saccades training exercise:
1. Hold two thumbs up, 60cm apart
2. Keep your head still
3. Look rapidly between left thumb → right thumb
4. 20 reps, then rest 10 seconds
5. Repeat 3 sets

You can feel the difference in reading speed within 10 sessions.

Guided version with audio timing at axeo.vision/exercise

#Saccades #EyeExercise #ReadingSpeed #Axeo #AxeoVision #SportsVision #EyeHealth

posts.hook: Week 3 eye health check-in — what was the most surprising thing you learned this week?
posts.caption:
r/EyeCare or r/HealthyHabits

End of another week. Running a check-in:

This week I went deep on vision testing science. A few things that genuinely surprised me:

1. Contrast sensitivity — I had no idea this was a separate, measurable skill from acuity. Took the free test at axeo.vision and found I'm slightly below average for my age group. Explains my discomfort with certain color combinations on screens.

2. Convergence insufficiency — the ADHD misdiagnosis connection is real and documented. I had a lot of the symptoms as a kid that were attributed to 'not trying hard enough to read.'

3. The Amsler grid — 30 seconds, free, browser-based, can detect early macular changes. This has been an ophthalmologist recommendation for home monitoring for decades. Why has no one made it easily available online until now?

Anyone else doing weekly vision tracking? Curious what others are noticing in their results over time.

All the tests I've been using: axeo.vision (free, no signup).
posts.visual_notes: Authentic community check-in. Three personal observations. Genuinely curious and educational. Soft Axeo mention through usage context.
